question: Describe the composition of the ECCS (Emergency Core Cooling System).

nuscale_ch01_0188.title: BTP 6-1, (March 2007): pH for Emergency Coolant Water for Pressurized Water ReactorsB.3 Hydrogen Generation from Aluminum Corrosion
nuscale_ch01_0188.summary: The section explains NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria regarding pH control and hydrogen generation from aluminum corrosion in emergency coolant water. While some criteria reference systems not present in the NuScale design (such as containment spray systems or sumps), the intent of the guidelines is still met due to similar water recirculation processes within the containment vessel. Other criteria, such as those related to minimum containment pressure and dual containment leakage, are not applicable to the NuScale design.

nuscale_ch01_0189.title: BTP 6-2, Rev 3: Minimum Containment Pressure Model for PWR ECCS PerformanceAll (B.1 thru B.3)Various
nuscale_ch01_0189.summary: This section explains that the guidance in BTP 6-2, Rev 3, regarding minimum containment pressure models for PWR ECCS performance, is not applicable to the NuScale design because a postulated LOCA does not result in core uncovery. Therefore, the regulatory criteria addressed in this BTP do not apply to NuScale’s containment and ECCS configuration.

nuscale_ch01_0017.title: Facility Description
nuscale_ch01_0017.summary: The Facility Description section outlines the NuScale plant’s core and cooling system design. It explains the natural circulation process within the reactor, the generation of superheated steam to drive a turbine and generator, and the role of the Site Cooling Water System (SCWS) in supporting auxiliary systems and heat removal. Equipment layout and operation details are site-specific, with further information provided in referenced sections.

nuscale_ch01_0024.title: Emergency Core Cooling System
nuscale_ch01_0024.summary: The Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) in the NuScale design is a fully passive system that removes decay heat during a loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA) using three independent reactor vent valves and two recirculation valves, all of which are closed during normal operation. During an emergency, the system vents steam into containment, condenses it, and recirculates water to cool the core, with heat ultimately transferred passively to the reactor pool.

nuscale_ch01_0063.title: Emergency Core Cooling System Valve Design Certification Application Demonstration Test
nuscale_ch01_0063.summary: This section describes the demonstration test program for the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) valves in the NuScale reactor design. The program aimed to verify the functional performance of the ECCS valves—including their operation at reactor pressures and temperatures, and in borated coolant—using test setups that replicated actual system conditions and included unique design features like the inadvertent actuation block (IAB). Testing was conducted at a specialized facility using a representative valve assembly to ensure the valves met safety and operational requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0027.title: Inservice Inspection of Class 2 and 3 Components
nuscale_ch01_0027.summary: This section describes the passive safety systems of the NuScale plant, including the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS), which passively removes decay heat during a loss-of-coolant accident using independent valves and heat conduction to the reactor pool. It also outlines the Control Room Habitability System (CRHS), which supplies clean air to operators during emergencies, and notes that fission product control relies on the containment vessel and isolation system.

edges.description: Section A discusses the chemical and pH requirements for ECCS water, while Section B addresses the minimum containment pressure modeling for ECCS performance, both referencing aspects of ECCS operation and referencing the same regulatory guidance (BTP 6-1), thus connecting through shared regulatory criteria for ECCS design and operation.

Section A discusses the chemical and operational requirements for the ECCS water, while Section B provides a general description of the facility and its cooling systems, with both sections connected through their relevance to the overall plant cooling and safety systems but without direct specification of ECCS composition.

Section B provides a general description of the ECCS composition, while Section A specifies regulatory criteria and water chemistry requirements that partially apply to the ECCS as described in Section B.

Section A discusses the chemical and water chemistry requirements relevant to ECCS operation, while Section B describes the physical design and testing of ECCS valves, together providing complementary details about the ECCS's composition in terms of both chemical and mechanical aspects.

Section A discusses regulatory criteria and conformance related to the ECCS, while Section B provides a general facility and system description, including the cooling water system, both contributing information necessary to describe the ECCS composition.

Section A references regulatory criteria and partial conformance regarding ECCS operation and water collection in containment, while Section B provides the specific design composition of the ECCS (valves and their functions), thus Section B specifies the details for the general regulatory context in Section A.
Section B provides specific details about the ECCS valve components and their testing, which supports the general regulatory conformance discussion about ECCS in Section A.
Section B provides specific details about the ECCS valve components and their tested operability, which elaborates on the general plant and system overview in Section A.
Section A describes the components and configuration of the ECCS, while Section B provides test results confirming the operability and design features of the ECCS valves mentioned in Section A.

Section A describes the physical components and operation of the ECCS, while Section B discusses regulatory criteria for water chemistry during ECCS operation, connecting them through the operational behavior and requirements of the ECCS water.

Section A describes the physical components and operation of the ECCS, while Section B discusses regulatory criteria for ECCS water chemistry and containment features, referencing how the NuScale ECCS design differs from traditional requirements.

Section A specifically describes the components and function of the ECCS, while Section B provides a general facility and process overview without detailing the ECCS, so Section A provides details/specifications for the more general context in Section B.
